text: After division of the inferior pulmonary ligament and harvest of level 9 lymph nodes, we confirmed that the mass was encasing the left inferior pulmonary vein.
At this point, the pericardium was opened, initially with a long bipolar grasper, followed by the vessel sealer anterior to the phrenic neurovascular bundle.
The dissection was then continued posteriorly to identify the pulmonary vein using a mix of blunt and bipolar dissection.
During this step, the PET-SIDA system keeps the field clear of pericardial fluid with the robotic suction irrigator.
The single pulmonary vein variant is visualized here, and the inferior branch is dissected first from the caudal edge using the long bipolar forceps.
Next, the confluence of the superior and inferior veins is dissected anteriorly to gain control of the inferior pulmonary vein.
For the posterior interpericardial dissection, we switch suction irrigator in arm one and a tip of grasper in arm three.
which allowed to bluntly encircle the vein until the grasper was able to pass through without resistance.
This was also confirmed by passing an endolator between the vein and the left lower lobe bronchus, which was able to go through without any resistance.
After the vein was fully dissected, the major fissure was opened using bipolar energy and the superior segment pulmonary artery was dissected.
It was further encircled with a vessel loop and then divided using a vascular staple loop.
Dissection was then carried anteriorly to the basilar trunk, and this was divided in a similar fashion.
Next, the left inferior pulmonary vein that was already dissected was divided also with a vascular staple load.
Finally, the left lower lobe bronchus was dissected and divided using a green staple lobe.
It can be seen here some of the challenges in retracting the left lower lobe, given the size of the mass, which is why we performed an anterior approach first.
After this step, we completed a mediastinal and hilar limb node harvest, extracted the specimen, and completed the procedure.
